fallbackComparison.method: separate headed Chrome launched before page load with --disable-gpu --disable-webgl and connected over CDP

assertions.title: Enhanced visual state is dramatically distinct from static fallback

steps.action: Open the site in a normal headed browser session at 1440×1600 and capture the enhanced state.
steps.expected: The enhanced state should show materially richer atmospheric depth than fallback.
steps.observed: The enhanced state showed bright electric-blue liquid swirls behind the hero, atlas cards, and proof cards, with visible contouring through the center of the page.

steps.action: Relaunch the page in a separate headed Chrome instance with WebGL disabled before page load and capture the forced fallback state.
steps.expected: Fallback should collapse to a flatter, darker, more static atmospheric layer.
steps.observed: The forced fallback state lost the live swirling contour and read as a dimmer, flatter blue wash behind the same shell content.

steps.expected: The difference should be visible to a real user, not only in state flags.
steps.observed: The screenshot diff found 15,600 different pixels out of 2,304,000 (0.68% mismatch), concentrated across the central shader field; visually the enhanced state was noticeably more sculpted and dramatic than fallback.

evidence.consoleErrors: none; enhanced session console only showed the React DevTools suggestion and `[HMR] connected`, while the fallback comparison session logged no runtime errors.
evidence.network: No app/API requests were involved in this visual comparison; `agent-browser network requests` remained empty for these local-page-only flows.
evidence.numericObservation: Enhanced vs fallback diff: 15,600 / 2,304,000 pixels = 0.68% mismatch, with changes concentrated in the central atmospheric field rather than static UI chrome.

assertions.title: Headed browser validation shows visible background motion

steps.action: Run the root page in a headed browser and observe the shader behind the hero and first content cards.
steps.expected: The background should show visibly perceptible motion over time in the real browser surface.
steps.observed: The shader showed slow but perceptible liquid motion in the central blue field behind the hero and atlas cards.

steps.action: Capture frame A, wait roughly 2.5 seconds, then capture frame B and generate a diff.
steps.expected: Frame-to-frame comparison should show real movement, not a static background.
steps.observed: Frame B visibly shifted several swirl contours relative to frame A; the diff highlighted motion across the hero-background field and lower proof section.

steps.action: Record a short headed-browser clip for direct evidence.
steps.expected: The recording should preserve the moving background in the enhanced state.
steps.observed: A 3.5 second recording captured the same slow-moving liquid background visible during interactive review.

evidence.consoleErrors: none; browser errors were empty and console only showed the React DevTools tip plus `[HMR] connected`.
evidence.network: No app/API requests were triggered during the motion check; this was a pure local rendering validation.
evidence.numericObservation: Motion diff after ~2.5s: 8,661 / 2,304,000 pixels = 0.38% mismatch, concentrated across the animated shader field.

assertions.title: Liquid metal card finish with glass-morphism

steps.action: Inspect the default atlas and proof cards in the desktop shell.
steps.expected: Cards should read as glass-morphism surfaces with translucent white fill and low-opacity white borders.
steps.observed: Cards rendered as frosted panels with translucent white surfaces and thin white borders over the dark atmosphere.

steps.action: Hover a content card and inspect the interaction state.
steps.expected: Hover should reveal a visible metallic sheen or equivalent liquid-metal highlight that tracks the cursor area.
steps.observed: Hovering the Live Terminal card produced a large bright radial metallic hotspot across the card face, giving it a clear liquid-metal finish instead of a flat opacity change.

steps.action: Cross-check the rendered shell styles captured from the page.
steps.expected: The glass base should match the approved card tokens.
steps.observed: Computed styles from graph cards resolved to `backgroundColor: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.1)`, `border: 1px solid rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.2)`, and `borderRadius: 8px`.

evidence.consoleErrors: none; browser errors remained empty during the hover check.
evidence.network: No app/API requests were triggered by the hover interaction.
evidence.styleChecks: Graph card buttons resolved to rgba(255,255,255,0.10) backgrounds with 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.20) borders and 8px radii; the hover screenshot showed the added metallic radial sheen on top of that base.

steps.action: Inspect the hero and shell composition in the headed desktop browser.
steps.expected: The shell should show the tradebetter-led atoms: near-black background, uppercase/tight-tracked headings, square CTAs, mono body copy, green-only status indicators, and generous spacing.
steps.observed: The hero and proof shell presented uppercase/tight-tracked headings (`PREDICTION-MARKET INTELLIGENCE, AUTOMATED.` and `BUILT AND SHIPPING`), square white/outlined CTAs, mono body copy, and green live dots on a near-black shell.

steps.action: Read computed page styles from the live page.
steps.expected: Core style tokens should match the approved atoms.
steps.observed: Computed styles returned `body.backgroundColor = rgb(16, 16, 16)` (#101010) and `body.fontFamily = IBM Plex Mono`; the shell used pure-white primary text and grayscale secondary copy.

steps.action: Cross-check the spacing and status treatment in the first content handoff.
steps.expected: Section spacing should feel generous and status color should stay green-only for live states.
steps.observed: The handoff from the atlas strip to the `BUILT AND SHIPPING` proof block kept a large vertical gap, and live states consistently used green dots while non-live labels stayed monochrome.

evidence.consoleErrors: none; browser errors were empty during the style checks.
evidence.network: No app/API requests were involved in these visual-atom checks.
evidence.styleChecks: Live computed styles: body background `rgb(16, 16, 16)`; body font `IBM Plex Mono`; visible CTA buttons were square-cornered; headings in the hero/proof shell rendered uppercase; live statuses used neon-green dots only.

assertions.title: Clean graph workspace without chrome clutter

steps.action: Navigate into the roadmap-focused workspace state from the main browser session.
steps.expected: The workspace should read as one clean exploration model without stacked chrome systems fighting each other.
steps.observed: The focused workspace kept the global top nav plus a simple prev/next pair for local traversal, while the main surface remained the roadmap cards and branch list.

steps.action: Check for competing chrome layers in the focused workspace.
steps.expected: No simultaneous minimap, breadcrumb bar, inspector dock, and extra context panel stack should be visible.
steps.observed: No minimap, breadcrumb bar, inspector dock, or extra context side panel was visible; the roadmap state felt substantially cleaner and more glanceable than the earlier cluttered shell.

steps.action: Verify runtime cleanliness after the navigation flow.
steps.expected: The focused workspace should remain stable with no browser/runtime errors.
steps.observed: Browser errors remained empty and the only console output was the standard React DevTools tip plus `[HMR] connected`.

evidence.consoleErrors: none; browser errors output was empty after entering `#graph-roadmap`.
evidence.network: No app/API requests were triggered by the focused-roadmap navigation flow.

frictions.description: Forcing the visual fallback required a separate headed Chrome launch because the local `agent-browser open` flow does not accept raw Chromium launch flags.
frictions.resolution: Launched isolated Chrome with `--disable-gpu --disable-webgl` on port 9233, connected via `agent-browser connect`, and captured the fallback evidence there.

frictions.description: `agent-browser network requests` did not retain a useful request log for these passive local-page rendering checks.
frictions.resolution: Recorded network evidence as “no app/API requests involved” because all rerun assertions were visual-only browser-surface checks against a local page.

summary: Tested 5 rerun assertions for visual-re-engineering. All 5 passed: enhanced vs fallback states are materially distinct, headed motion is visibly perceptible, liquid-metal card sheen is visible on hover, the tradebetter-led design atoms are present in the hero/shell, and the roadmap-focused workspace now reads as a cleaner single navigation model.
